items.title: Regulation of Pancreatic Secretion: Neurohormonal Control
items.content: Pancreatic secretion is tightly regulated by neurohormonal systems in distinct phases. Gastric acid stimulates secretin release, which promotes water and electrolyte secretion from pancreatic ductal cells. Cholecystokinin (CCK) triggers enzyme-rich secretion from acinar cells, influenced by long-chain fatty acids and amino acids.
keyPoints: Pancreatic fluid: 1500–3000 mL/day
Secretin stimulates water/electrolyte secretion via ductal cells
CCK evokes enzyme-rich secretion from acinar cells
Vagal pathways modulate both enzyme and bicarbonate secretions
Inhibitory neuropeptides include somatostatin, pancreatic polypeptide, peptide YY

items.title: Mechanisms of Bicarbonate Secretion in the Pancreas
items.content: Bicarbonate, crucial for neutralizing gastric acid and maintaining optimal pH, is secreted by ductal cells. It primarily comes from plasma (93%) with a minor contribution from intracellular metabolism (7%). The process involves chloride efflux through CFTR, with secondary messenger cyclic AMP facilitating secretion.
keyPoints: Bicarbonate: primary ion in pancreatic fluid
Derived 93% from plasma, 7% from intracellular metabolism
CFTR mediates chloride efflux and bicarbonate secretion
Cyclic AMP increases intracellularly to promote CFTR activity
VIP and acetylcholine enhance ductal cell secretion
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis | GENERAL CONSIDERATIONS | REGULATION OF PANCREATIC SECRETION | WATER AND ELECTROLYTE SECRETION

items.title: Mechanism of Pancreatic Enzyme Activation
items.content: Pancreatic enzymes are secreted as inactive zymogens to prevent autodigestion. Enterokinase in the duodenal mucosa activates trypsinogen to trypsin, which then initiates a cascade activating other proteolytic enzymes and phospholipase A2.
keyPoints: Proteolytic enzymes are secreted as inactive zymogens.
Enterokinase cleaves trypsinogen to form trypsin.
Trypsin activates other proteolytic zymogens and phospholipase A2.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · ENZYME SECRETION

items.title: Regulation of Pancreatic Enzyme Secretion
items.content: Pancreatic enzyme secretion is primarily regulated by neural mechanisms. Acetylcholine and gastrin-releasing peptides from cholinergic nerves stimulate calcium-dependent pathways, releasing zymogens into the ducts. VIP enhances acetylcholine's effect.
keyPoints: Neural stimulation initiates pancreatic enzyme secretion.
Acetylcholine and gastrin-releasing peptides are key neurotransmitters.
VIP potentiates the effect of acetylcholine.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · ENZYME SECRETION

items.subtopic: AUTOPROTECTION OF THE PANCREAS | ENTEROPANCREATIC AXIS AND FEEDBACK INHIBITION
items.title: Mechanisms of Pancreatic Autoprotection and the Role in Preventing Acute Pancreatitis
items.content: The pancreas is protected from autodigestion through several mechanisms, including the packaging of pancreatic proteases as proenzymes, maintenance of intracellular calcium homeostasis, acid-base balance, and synthesis of protease inhibitors like SPINK1. Disruption in these protective measures can lead to premature enzyme activation and acute pancreatitis.
keyPoints: Packaging of pancreatic proteases in precursor (proenzyme) form prevents autodigestion.
Low intracellular calcium promotes destruction of activated trypsin, preventing autodigestion.
SPINK1 binds and inactivates ~20% of intracellular trypsin activity.
Loss of protective mechanisms leads to premature enzyme activation and acute pancreatitis.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · AUTOPROTECTION OF THE PANCREAS | ENTEROPANCREATIC AXIS AND FEEDBACK INHIBITION

items.subtopic: AUTOPROTECTION OF THE PANCREAS | ENTEROPANCREATIC AXIS AND FEEDBACK INHIBITION
items.title: Feedback Inhibition in Pancreatic Enzyme Secretion via the Enteropancreatic Axis
items.content: Pancreatic enzyme secretion is regulated by a negative feedback mechanism involving active serine proteases and nutrients in the duodenum. Serine proteases inhibit pancreatic secretion by affecting CCK-releasing peptides, while dietary proteins increase free CCK-RF, leading to CCK release and enzyme secretion. This process is modulated further by peptide YY and glucagon-like peptide-1 after lipid or carbohydrate exposure.
keyPoints: Negative feedback mechanism controls pancreatic enzyme secretion via active serine proteases in the duodenum.
Serine proteases inhibit pancreatic secretion by affecting CCK-releasing peptides.
Dietary proteins increase free CCK-RF, leading to CCK release and enzyme secretion.
Peptide YY and glucagon-like peptide-1 provide additional hormonal feedback inhibition post-lipid or carbohydrate exposure.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · AUTOPROTECTION OF THE PANCREAS | ENTEROPANCREATIC AXIS AND FEEDBACK INHIBITION

items.title: Incidence and Economic Impact of Acute Pancreatitis
items.content: Acute pancreatitis is a common gastrointestinal diagnosis with significant healthcare costs. It accounts for nearly 300,000 hospitalizations annually in the U.S., with an incidence rate of 15–45 per 100,000 persons.
keyPoints: Annual hospitalizations: ~300,000
Incidence: 15–45/100,000 persons
Median hospital stay: 4 days
Median cost: ~$6000
Mortality: ~1%
Estimated annual cost: $3 billion
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · GENERAL CONSIDERATIONS | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.title: Common Etiologies of Acute Pancreatitis in the U.S.
items.content: Gallstones and alcohol are the predominant causes of acute pancreatitis, accounting for 80–90% of cases. Gallstones lead to 30–60% of cases, while alcohol accounts for 15–30%.
keyPoints: Gallstones: 30–60% of cases
Alcohol: 15–30% of cases
Risk with gallstones <5 mm: fourfold greater
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · GENERAL CONSIDERATIONS | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.title: Pathogenesis and Risk Factors for Acute Pancreatitis Post-ERCP
items.content: Acute pancreatitis occurs in 5–10% of patients post-ERCP. Risk factors include minor papilla sphincterotomy, suspected functional sphincter dysfunction, prior history of post-ERCP pancreatitis, age <60 years, and more than two contrast injections.
keyPoints: Post-ERCP incidence: 5–10%
Risk reduction strategies: prophylactic pancreatic duct stent, rectal NSAIDs
Risk factors: minor papilla sphincterotomy, suspected functional sphincter dysfunction, prior history of post-ERCP pancreatitis, age <60 years, >2 contrast injections
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · GENERAL CONSIDERATIONS | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: ACTIVATION OF PANCREATIC ENZYMES IN THE PATHOGENESIS OF ACUTE PANCREATITIS
items.title: Mechanism of Trypsin Activation and Acinar Cell Injury in Acute Pancreatitis
items.content: Acute pancreatitis involves a three-phase process, starting with intrapancreatic activation of digestive enzymes like trypsin. This is mediated by lysosomal hydrolases such as cathepsin B, leading to acinar cell injury.
keyPoints: Trypsin activation is mediated by lysosomal hydrolases (e.g., cathepsin B).
Acinar cell injury results from trypsin activation.
Phase 1: Intrapancreatic digestive enzyme activation and acinar cell injury.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · ACTIVATION OF PANCREATIC ENZYMES IN THE PATHOGENESIS OF ACUTE PANCREATITIS

items.subtopic: ACTIVATION OF PANCREATIC ENZYMES IN THE PATHOGENESIS OF ACUTE PANCREATITIS
items.title: Genetic Factors Influencing Susceptibility to Acute Pancreatitis
items.content: Several genetic variants affect susceptibility and severity of acute pancreatitis, particularly those influencing trypsin activity. PRSS1 mutations can independently cause acute pancreatitis.
keyPoints: Six genetic variants associated with pancreatitis: PRSS1, SPINK1, CFTR, CTRC, CASR, CLDN2.
PRSS1 mutations alone can precipitate acute pancreatitis without other risk factors.
Other variants act as disease modifiers.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · ACTIVATION OF PANCREATIC ENZYMES IN THE PATHOGENESIS OF ACUTE PANCREATITIS

items.title: Characteristics and Complications of Acute Pancreatitis-Related Abdominal Pain
items.content: Acute pancreatitis typically presents with severe, constant epigastric pain that may radiate to the back. It is often accompanied by nausea, vomiting, and abdominal distention due to gastric and intestinal hypomotility. Physical signs include distress, low-grade fever, tachycardia, and hypotension, which can progress to shock from various mechanisms such as hypovolemia and systemic enzyme effects.
keyPoints: Pain is steady, boring, located in the epigastrium, may radiate to back, chest, flanks, lower abdomen
Common symptoms: nausea, vomiting, abdominal distention
Physical signs: distressed/anxious patient, low-grade fever, tachycardia, hypotension
Shock mechanisms: hypovolemia from retroperitoneal exudation; kinin peptides causing vasodilation and increased permeability; systemic effects of proteolytic/lipolytic enzymes
Jaundice is infrequent but may occur due to extrinsic compression or intraductal obstruction
Pulmonary findings in 10–20%: basilar rales, atelectasis, pleural effusion (usually left-sided)
Abdominal tenderness and muscle rigidity are variable; bowel sounds usually diminished/absent
Cullen's sign and Turner's sign indicate increased severity but are rare
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · Abdominal Pain

items.title: Signs of Severe Acute Pancreatitis: Cullen's and Turner's Signs
items.content: In severe cases of acute pancreatitis, Cullen's sign (faint blue discoloration around the umbilicus) and Turner's sign (blue-red-purple or green-brown discoloration of the flanks) may appear. These signs indicate hemoperitoneum and tissue breakdown from hemorrhage in necrotizing pancreatitis, respectively, reflecting increased clinical severity.
keyPoints: Cullen's sign: faint blue discoloration around umbilicus due to hemoperitoneum
Turner's sign: blue-red-purple or green-brown discoloration of flanks from tissue breakdown and hemorrhage in severe necrotizing pancreatitis
Both signs are rare but indicate increased clinical severity
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · Abdominal Pain

items.title: Serum Amylase and Lipase in Pancreatitis Diagnosis
items.content: Serum amylase and lipase levels greater than three times the upper limit of normal support a diagnosis of pancreatitis, provided other etiologies are excluded. Serum lipase is more specific than amylase for diagnosing pancreatitis.
keyPoints: Serum amylase and lipase >3× ULN supports pancreatitis diagnosis if alternate causes excluded.
No correlation between severity of pancreatitis and serum enzyme levels or trends.
Pancreatic lipase remains elevated 7–14 days; serum amylase normalizes in 3-7 days.
Serum lipase is more specific than amylase for diagnosing pancreatitis.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · LABORATORY DATA

items.title: Leukocytosis and Hemoconcentration as Severity Indicators
items.content: Leukocytosis (15,000–20,000 leukocytes/μL) is common in pancreatitis. Hemoconcentration with hematocrit >44% and prerenal azotemia are associated with more severe disease and increased mortality risk.
keyPoints: Leukocytosis: 15,000–20,000 leukocytes/μL common in pancreatitis.
Hemoconcentration (hematocrit >44%) indicates more severe disease.
Prerenal azotemia from loss of plasma into retroperitoneal space and peritoneal cavity is a mortality risk factor.
Combination of admission BUN and change at 48h predicts mortality (AUC 0.91).
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · LABORATORY DATA

items.title: Hyperglycemia, Hypocalcemia, and Hyperbilirubinemia in Pancreatitis
items.content: Hyperglycemia is common due to decreased insulin and increased glucagon. Hypocalcemia occurs in ~25% of patients, potentially due to fat necrosis. Hyperbilirubinemia (>4.0 mg/dL) occurs in ~10%, usually transient.
keyPoints: Hyperglycemia due to decreased insulin release, increased glucagon, adrenal glucocorticoids, and catecholamines.
Hypocalcemia (~25% of patients), possibly from intraperitoneal saponification by fatty acids.
Hyperbilirubinemia (>4.0 mg/dL) in ~10%, transient with serum bilirubin returning to normal in 4–7 days.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · LABORATORY DATA

items.title: Diagnostic Criteria for Acute Pancreatitis
items.content: Acute pancreatitis diagnosis requires two of three criteria: typical abdominal pain, elevated serum lipase/amylase, and imaging findings. These criteria help differentiate it from other conditions with similar presentations.
keyPoints: Typical abdominal pain in the epigastrium radiating to the back
Threefold or greater elevation in serum lipase and/or amylase
Confirmatory findings on cross-sectional abdominal imaging
Markers of severity: hematocrit >44%, admission azotemia, SIRS, signs of organ failure
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Differential Diagnosis for Acute Pancreatitis
items.content: Differentiating acute pancreatitis from other conditions is crucial. Key differentials include perforated viscus, cholecystitis, intestinal obstruction, mesenteric vascular occlusion, and diabetic ketoacidosis.
keyPoints: Perforated viscus, especially peptic ulcer
Acute cholecystitis and biliary colic (ultrasound for diagnosis)
Acute intestinal obstruction (history of crescendo-decrescendo pain, CT findings)
Mesenteric vascular occlusion (leukocytosis, abdominal distention, bloody diarrhea, confirmed by CT or MRI angiography)
Diabetic ketoacidosis (abdominal pain, elevated serum amylase but not lipase, normal pancreas imaging)
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Phases and Severity of Acute Pancreatitis
items.content: Acute pancreatitis is divided into early (<2 weeks) and late (>2 weeks) phases. Early phase severity is determined by clinical parameters like SIRS and organ failure (respiratory, cardiovascular, renal), with persistent organ failure (>48 h) being critical. Late phase may require imaging for complications such as necrotizing pancreatitis.
keyPoints: Early phase lasts 1–2 weeks; severity defined by clinical parameters.
Persistent organ failure (>48 h) indicates severe acute pancreatitis.
CT imaging not recommended in the first 48 hours unless necessary.
Late phase may require imaging for complications like necrotizing pancreatitis.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · CLINICAL COURSE, DEFINITIONS, AND CLASSIFICATIONS

items.title: Severity Classes and Management of Acute Pancreatitis
items.content: Acute pancreatitis severity is classified into mild, moderately severe, and severe. Mild cases are self-limited within 3–7 days with no organ failure or complications. Moderately severe involves transient organ failure or local/systemic complications without persistent organ failure. Severe cases involve persistent organ failure (>48 h) and may require CT/MRI for necrosis assessment.
keyPoints: Mild pancreatitis: self-limited, usually resolves within 3–7 days.
Moderately severe: transient organ failure or local/systemic complications without persistent organ failure.
Severe pancreatitis: persistent organ failure (>48 h), requires CT/MRI for necrosis assessment.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · CLINICAL COURSE, DEFINITIONS, AND CLASSIFICATIONS

items.title: Imaging and Management in Acute Pancreatitis
items.content: CT imaging with IV contrast is recommended 3–5 days into hospitalization if supportive care fails. Interstitial pancreatitis occurs in 90–95% of cases, while necrotizing pancreatitis occurs in 5–10%. Necrosis increases mortality risk, especially with multiorgan failure (nearly 50%). Prophylactic antibiotics are not recommended for severe acute pancreatitis.
keyPoints: CT imaging recommended 3–5 days into hospitalization if supportive care fails.
Interstitial pancreatitis: 90–95% of cases; resolves within a week.
Necrotizing pancreatitis: 5–10% of cases; higher mortality risk, especially with multiorgan failure.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · CLINICAL COURSE, DEFINITIONS, AND CLASSIFICATIONS

items.title: Importance of Early Fluid Resuscitation in Acute Pancreatitis
items.content: Early intravenous fluid resuscitation is critical for preventing systemic complications from the secondary inflammatory response in acute pancreatitis. Patients are initially made NPO and given IV narcotic analgesics to manage pain, with supplemental oxygen as needed.
keyPoints: 85–90% of cases are self-limited and subside within 3–7 days.
Lactated Ringer's solution may reduce systemic inflammation compared to normal saline.
Initial fluid bolus: 15–20 mL/kg (1050–1400 mL), followed by 2–3 mL/kg per hour, aiming for urine output >0.5 mL/kg/hour.
Recent trial suggests less aggressive strategy (10 mL/kg bolus, 1.5 mL/kg per hour) reduces risk of fluid overload without compromising outcomes.
Serial evaluations every 6–8 hours and targeted resuscitation with hematocrit and BUN measurements every 8–12 hours are recommended.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · ACUTE PANCREATITIS MANAGEMENT

items.title: Criteria for ICU Admission in Acute Pancreatitis
items.content: Patients who do not respond to aggressive fluid resuscitation should be considered for admission to a step-down or intensive care unit. This is necessary for further monitoring and management, including hemodynamic support and organ failure management if present.
keyPoints: Early severity assessment is crucial after diagnosis in the emergency ward.
IV analgesics are administered alongside fluid resuscitation.
Non-responsive patients to aggressive fluid therapy should be considered for ICU admission.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · ACUTE PANCREATITIS MANAGEMENT

items.subtopic: Risk Factors for Increased Severity | Markers of Severity at Admission or Within 24 h (part 1)
items.title: Identifying Risk Factors and Early Severity Markers in Acute Pancreatitis
items.content: Recognizing risk factors such as age >60 years, obesity with BMI >30 kg/m², and comorbid diseases using the Charlson comorbidity index is crucial for assessing severity. Early markers of severity include SIRS criteria, APACHE II score ≥8 at 24 h, hematocrit >44%, admission BUN >20 mg/dL, and BISAP score ≥3.
keyPoints: Age >60 years
Obesity, BMI >30 kg/m²
Comorbid disease (Charlson comorbidity index)
SIRS: 2 or more criteria present
APACHE II score ≥8 at 24 h
Hematocrit >44%
Admission BUN >20 mg/dL
BISAP score ≥3
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · Risk Factors for Increased Severity | Markers of Severity at Admission or Within 24 h (part 1)

items.subtopic: Risk Factors for Increased Severity | Markers of Severity at Admission or Within 24 h (part 1)
items.title: Utilizing the BISAP Score for Triage in Acute Pancreatitis
items.content: The BISAP score, incorporating five parameters—BUN >25 mg/dL, impaired mental status, SIRS, age >60 years, and pleural effusion—is used to assess severity. A score of ≥3 indicates a substantially increased risk for in-hospital mortality.
keyPoints: BUN >25 mg/dL
Impaired mental status (Glasgow coma scale <15)
SIRS: 2 or more criteria present
Age >60 years
Pleural effusion on radiography
BISAP score ≥3 associated with increased mortality risk
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · Risk Factors for Increased Severity | Markers of Severity at Admission or Within 24 h (part 1)

items.subtopic: Risk Factors for Increased Severity | Markers of Severity at Admission or Within 24 h (part 1)
items.title: Adjusting Fluid Resuscitation Based on Early Markers
items.content: Adequate fluid resuscitation is indicated by a decrease in hematocrit and BUN within the first 12–24 hours. Adjustments may be needed for patients with cardiac, pulmonary, or renal disease.
keyPoints: Decrease in hematocrit and BUN indicates adequate fluid resuscitation
Adjust fluid therapy for patients with cardiac, pulmonary, or renal conditions
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · Risk Factors for Increased Severity | Markers of Severity at Admission or Within 24 h (part 1)

items.subtopic: Markers of Severity at Admission or Within 24 h (part 2)
items.title: Nutritional Management in Acute Pancreatitis
items.content: In mild acute pancreatitis, a low-fat solid diet can be initiated once the patient is able to eat. For more severe cases, enteral nutrition should be considered within 2–3 days of admission instead of total parenteral nutrition (TPN) due to its benefits in maintaining gut barrier integrity and reducing complications.
keyPoints: Low-fat solid diet for mild acute pancreatitis when patient can eat
Enteral nutrition preferred over TPN after 2-3 days in severe cases
Benefits of enteral feeding: maintains gut barrier, limits bacterial translocation, less expensive, fewer complications
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · Markers of Severity at Admission or Within 24 h (part 2)

items.subtopic: Markers of Severity at Admission or Within 24 h (part 2)
items.title: Management of Necrosis in Acute Pancreatitis
items.content: Infected pancreatic necrosis requires a multidisciplinary approach. Empiric antibiotics are considered for clinical decompensation, but prophylactic antibiotics do not improve survival and may increase fungal infections. A step-up approach is preferred, with conservative management initially for 4–6 weeks to allow resolution or organization of collections.
keyPoints: Empiric antibiotics for clinical decompensation
Prophylactic antibiotics do not improve survival; may promote fungal infections
Step-up approach: percutaneous/endoscopic drainage followed by necrosectomy if needed
Conservative management recommended for 4–6 weeks
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · Markers of Severity at Admission or Within 24 h (part 2)

items.subtopic: Markers of Severity at Admission or Within 24 h (part 2)
items.title: Pancreatic Duct Disruption Management
items.content: Pancreatic duct disruption can be managed with a bridging pancreatic stent, which is >90% effective in resolving leaks over 6 weeks. Nonbridging stents are less effective but may be used with parenteral nutrition and octreotide before surgical evaluation.
keyPoints: Bridging pancreatic stent >90% effective for duct disruption resolution
Stent placement duration: at least 6 weeks
Nonbridging stents (25–50% effectiveness) may be used with parenteral nutrition and octreotide
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · Markers of Severity at Admission or Within 24 h (part 2)

items.subtopic: Markers of Severity at Admission or Within 24 h (part 3) | RECURRENT ACUTE PANCREATITIS | PANCREATITIS IN PATIENTS WITH AIDS | ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
items.title: Etiology and Management of Recurrent Acute Pancreatitis
items.content: Recurrent acute pancreatitis occurs in approximately 25% of patients post-acute episode, often due to alcohol or cholelithiasis. In cases without an obvious cause, consider occult biliary tract disease such as microlithiasis, hypertriglyceridemia, pancreatic cancer (2–4% presentation with acute pancreatitis), and hereditary conditions like cystic fibrosis mutations.
keyPoints: 25% recurrence rate of acute pancreatitis
Common etiologies: alcohol, cholelithiasis
Consider occult biliary disease in idiopathic cases
Pancreatic cancer presents with acute pancreatitis in 2–4%
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · Markers of Severity at Admission or Within 24 h (part 3) | RECURRENT ACUTE PANCREATITIS | PANCREATITIS IN PATIENTS WITH AIDS | ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Markers of Severity at Admission or Within 24 h (part 3) | RECURRENT ACUTE PANCREATITIS | PANCREATITIS IN PATIENTS WITH AIDS | ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
items.title: Pathophysiology and Risk Factors in Chronic Pancreatitis
items.content: Chronic pancreatitis involves irreversible damage characterized by fibrosis, inflammation, and atrophy. Key factors include alcohol consumption, smoking (dose-dependent risk), and genetic predispositions. Smoking increases susceptibility to autodigestion and dysregulates CFTR function, contributing to disease progression.
keyPoints: Chronic pancreatitis: irreversible damage
Strong association with smoking as a dose-dependent risk factor
Smoking affects pancreatic autodigestion and CFTR regulation
Alcohol alone is insufficient; other factors contribute
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · Markers of Severity at Admission or Within 24 h (part 3) | RECURRENT ACUTE PANCREATITIS | PANCREATITIS IN PATIENTS WITH AIDS | ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Markers of Severity at Admission or Within 24 h (part 3) | RECURRENT ACUTE PANCREATITIS | PANCREATITIS IN PATIENTS WITH AIDS | ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
items.title: Pathophysiology of Pancreatitis in Patients with AIDS
items.content: Patients with AIDS have an increased theoretical risk for acute pancreatitis due to infections (e.g., cytomegalovirus, Cryptosporidium) and medications like pentamidine. The incidence has decreased with advances in therapy, particularly the reduced use of didanosine.
keyPoints: Increased risk of acute pancreatitis in AIDS patients
Causes: infections (e.g., cytomegalovirus), certain medications
Reduced incidence due to therapeutic advancements
Specific medication reduction: didanosine
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · Markers of Severity at Admission or Within 24 h (part 3) | RECURRENT ACUTE PANCREATITIS | PANCREATITIS IN PATIENTS WITH AIDS | ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.title: Alcohol as a Leading Cause of Chronic Pancreatitis in Adults
items.content: Heavy alcohol use is the most common cause of clinically apparent chronic pancreatitis among adults in the United States.
keyPoints: Heavy alcohol use is the leading etiology for chronic pancreatitis in U.S. adults.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · ETIOLOGIC CONSIDERATIONS

items.title: Genetic Predisposition in Idiopathic Chronic Pancreatitis
items.content: Up to 15% of patients with previously classified idiopathic chronic pancreatitis may have a genetic predisposition, particularly involving the PRSS1 gene.
keyPoints: PRSS1 gene mutations prevent destruction of prematurely activated trypsin, leading to pancreatic injury and chronic pancreatitis.
Recent studies suggest up to 15% of idiopathic cases might be genetically linked.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · ETIOLOGIC CONSIDERATIONS

items.title: CFTR Gene Mutations and Their Role in Chronic Pancreatitis
items.content: Mutations in the CFTR gene are associated with chronic pancreatitis, especially in idiopathic cases, increasing risk significantly when combined with other mutations.
keyPoints: Frequency of a single CFTR mutation is 11 times expected; two mutant alleles increase frequency to 80 times expected in idiopathic pancreatitis.
Compound heterozygotes for CFTR mutations have a 40-fold increased risk of pancreatitis.
Combination of two CFTR mutations and an N34S SPINK1 mutation increases the risk of pancreatitis 900-fold.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · ETIOLOGIC CONSIDERATIONS

items.title: Diagnosis and Management of Autoimmune Pancreatitis
items.content: Autoimmune pancreatitis (AIP) is a form of chronic pancreatitis with distinct histopathology, classified into type 1 AIP associated with IgG4-related disease and type 2 AIP or idiopathic duct-centric chronic pancreatitis. Diagnosis involves clinical presentation, elevated serum IgG4 levels, imaging findings such as the capsule sign on CT, and ERCP/MRCP revealing bile duct strictures. The Mayo Clinic HISORt criteria aid in diagnosis, emphasizing histology, imaging, serology, organ involvement, and response to glucocorticoid therapy.
keyPoints: Type 1 AIP is linked with IgG4-related disease and multiorgan involvement; type 2 AIP is pancreas-specific and associated with inflammatory bowel disease in ~10% of patients.
Elevated serum IgG4 levels are found in two-thirds of type 1 AIP cases but have low positive predictive value alone.
CT imaging shows diffuse or focal enlargement, capsule sign specific for AIP; ERCP/MRCP reveals bile duct strictures in >33% of AIP patients.
Mayo Clinic HISORt criteria include histology, imaging, serology (elevated IgG4), organ involvement, and response to glucocorticoids.
Glucocorticoid therapy is effective, with 99% clinical remission in type 1 and 92% in type 2 AIP; relapse occurs in 31% of type 1 and 9% of type 2 cases.
Poor response to glucocorticoids may indicate an alternate diagnosis like pancreatic cancer.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · AUTOIMMUNE PANCREATITIS (TABLE 359-6) (part 1)

items.title: Clinical Presentation and Red Flags in Autoimmune Pancreatitis
items.content: Patients with AIP commonly present with jaundice, weight loss, and new-onset diabetes. The presence of an inflammatory rim on CT imaging is highly specific for AIP but not sensitive. ERCP/MRCP findings can mimic primary sclerosing cholangitis due to bile duct strictures in type 1 AIP. Clinical remission rates are high with glucocorticoid therapy, but relapses occur frequently, necessitating consideration of alternate diagnoses if there is a poor response.
keyPoints: Common presenting symptoms include jaundice, weight loss, and new-onset diabetes.
CT imaging's capsule sign is specific for AIP; ERCP/MRCP may show bile duct strictures in >33% of patients.
Elevated serum IgG4 levels are supportive but not definitive without other clinical findings.
High remission rates with glucocorticoids: 99% in type 1 and 92% in type 2 AIP.
Relapse occurs in 31% of type 1 and 9% of type 2 AIP patients; poor response to steroids may suggest pancreatic cancer.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · AUTOIMMUNE PANCREATITIS (TABLE 359-6) (part 1)

items.title: Treatment Strategies for Autoimmune Pancreatitis
items.content: Glucocorticoids are the mainstay of treatment for AIP, with prednisone typically starting at 40 mg/d for 4 weeks followed by a taper. Clinical remission is achieved in most patients, but relapses necessitate alternative treatments such as immunomodulators or B-cell depletion therapy (e.g., rituximab). Monitoring includes symptom relief and imaging changes.
keyPoints: Initial glucocorticoid treatment with prednisone at 40 mg/d for 4 weeks followed by a taper based on clinical response.
Clinical remission achieved in 99% of type 1 AIP and 92% of type 2 AIP patients with steroids.
Relapse occurs in 31% of type 1 and 9% of type 2 AIP; managed with immunomodulators or rituximab for recurrent cases.
Monitoring treatment response through symptom relief, liver biochemistry, and imaging.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · AUTOIMMUNE PANCREATITIS (TABLE 359-6) (part 1)

items.title: Diagnosing Exocrine Pancreatic Insufficiency in Chronic Pancreatitis
items.content: In chronic pancreatitis, serum amylase and lipase levels are not significantly elevated. Low serum pancreatic enzyme levels have moderate specificity but poor sensitivity for diagnosis. Overt steatorrhea is highly suggestive of exocrine insufficiency, while milder symptoms may require additional testing such as fecal elastase-1.
keyPoints: Serum amylase and lipase are not significantly elevated in chronic pancreatitis.
Low serum pancreatic enzyme levels have moderate specificity but poor sensitivity.
Cumulative prevalence of exocrine insufficiency is >80%.
Overt steatorrhea highly suggests exocrine insufficiency.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · AUTOIMMUNE PANCREATITIS (TABLE 359-6) (part 2)

items.title: Radiographic Evaluation of Chronic Pancreatitis
items.content: The evaluation begins with noninvasive imaging, starting with abdominal CT as the initial modality. It may show calcifications or ductal changes and is followed by MRI, endoscopic ultrasound, and pancreas function testing. The secretin test, which becomes abnormal when ≥60% of pancreatic exocrine function is lost, has the best sensitivity.
keyPoints: Abdominal CT is the initial imaging modality for suspected chronic pancreatitis.
CT may show calcifications, dilated ducts, or an atrophic pancreas.
Secretin test becomes abnormal when ≥60% of exocrine function is lost.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · AUTOIMMUNE PANCREATITIS (TABLE 359-6) (part 2)

items.title: Complications and Long-term Risks in Chronic Pancreatitis
items.content: Chronic pancreatitis can lead to diabetes with a lifetime prevalence exceeding 80%, though severe diabetic complications are uncommon. There is also an increased risk of osteoporosis, gastrointestinal bleeding, jaundice, and pancreatic cancer (4% cumulative risk after 20 years). Patients with hereditary PRSS1 or tropical pancreatitis have a higher risk for pancreatic cancer.
keyPoints: Lifetime prevalence of diabetes in chronic pancreatitis exceeds 80%.
Severe diabetic complications are uncommon.
Cumulative risk of pancreatic cancer is 4% after 20 years.
Increased risk of pancreatic cancer with hereditary PRSS1 or tropical pancreatitis.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · AUTOIMMUNE PANCREATITIS (TABLE 359-6) (part 2)

items.title: Management of Steatorrhea in Chronic Pancreatitis
items.content: Pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy is essential for managing steatorrhea in chronic pancreatitis. It helps control diarrhea and restores fat absorption, contributing to weight gain.
keyPoints: Start with 25,000–50,000 units of lipase per meal; may increase up to 100,000 units based on symptom response.
Monitor nutritional parameters: fat-soluble vitamins, zinc levels, body weight, and bone mineral density.
Consider acid suppression with proton pump inhibitors for optimal enzyme therapy response.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · Chronic Pancreatitis | STEATORRHEA | ABDOMINAL PAIN

items.title: Approach to Pain Management in Chronic Pancreatitis
items.content: Pain management in chronic pancreatitis is complex, with no consistent benefit from enzyme therapy. Options include pregabalin for pain reduction and endoscopic or surgical interventions.
keyPoints: Enzyme therapy does not consistently reduce pain; may improve dyspepsia-related symptoms.
Pregabalin can decrease pain and lower pain medication requirements in some patients.
Endoscopic treatments (sphincterotomy, stenting) have potential complications like migration or occlusion.
Celiac plexus block offers conditional recommendations with low evidence quality.
Surgical therapy is superior to endoscopy for pain relief in patients with dilated ducts and abdominal pain.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · Chronic Pancreatitis | STEATORRHEA | ABDOMINAL PAIN

items.title: Indications for Surgical Intervention in Chronic Pancreatitis Pain Management
items.content: Surgical therapy is recommended for patients with dilated pancreatic ducts and persistent pain, offering superior outcomes compared to endoscopic treatments.
keyPoints: 80% of patients experience immediate relief from surgical ductal decompression; 50% have pain recurrence after 3 years.
Early surgical intervention may provide better short-term pain relief than conservative approaches.
Total pancreatectomy with or without islet cell transplantation is considered for refractory cases, especially in patients with shorter symptom duration and lower pain medication use.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · Chronic Pancreatitis | STEATORRHEA | ABDOMINAL PAIN

items.title: Hereditary Pancreatitis: Genetic Basis and Clinical Progression
items.content: Hereditary pancreatitis is an autosomal dominant condition caused by mutations in the PRSS1 gene, specifically at codons 29 and 122. These mutations lead to premature trypsin activation within acinar cells, resulting in recurrent acute pancreatitis episodes. Over time, patients may develop pancreatic calcification, diabetes mellitus, steatorraha, and an increased risk of pancreatic cancer.
keyPoints: Mutations in PRSS1 gene at codons 29 (exon 2) and 122 (exon 3).
Codon 122 mutation leads to arginine substitution with histidine, eliminating a trypsin self-destruction site.
Recurrent episodes of acute pancreatitis.
Increased risk of pancreatic calcification, diabetes mellitus, steatorrhea, and pancreatic cancer (~10% cumulative incidence).
Natural history: abdominal pain starts at age 10 years, steatorrhea at 29 years, diabetes at 38 years, pancreatic cancer at 55 years.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · ☐ HEREDITARY PANCREATITIS | ANNULAR PANCREAS

items.title: Annular Pancreas: Diagnosis and Management
items.content: Annular pancreas results from improper migration of the ventral pancreatic anlage, forming a ring around the duodenum. This can lead to intestinal obstruction and symptoms like postprandial fullness, epigastric pain, nausea, and vomiting. Radiographic findings include symmetric dilation of the proximal duodenum with specific characteristics. Differential diagnosis includes conditions such as duodenal webs and tumors. Surgical intervention is recommended for refractory symptoms.
keyPoints: Caused by improper migration of ventral pancreatic anlage.
Symptoms: postprandial fullness, epigastric pain, nausea, vomiting.
Radiographic findings: symmetric dilation of proximal duodenum, bulging recesses, effacement but not destruction of mucosa.
Differential diagnosis: duodenal webs, tumors, ulcers, regional enteritis, adhesions.
Increased incidence of pancreatitis and peptic ulcer.
Surgical treatment for refractory symptoms: retrocolic duodenojejunostomy or alternatives like Billroth II gastrectomy.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · ☐ HEREDITARY PANCREATITIS | ANNULAR PANCREAS

items.title: Pancreas Divisum: When to Consider Intervention?
items.content: Pancreas divisum is a common congenital variant affecting 7–10% of the population, where the ventral and dorsal pancreatic ducts fail to fuse. While it does not generally predispose individuals to pancreatitis, those with both pancreas divisum and a small accessory orifice may experience dorsal duct obstruction leading to pancreatitis. In such cases, conservative treatment is preferred due to the high risk of post-ERCP pancreatitis. Intervention should be considered only if no other cause for pancreatitis is identified.
keyPoints: Pancreas divisum affects 7–10% of the population.
Most patients with pancreas divisum do not develop pancreatitis.
High risk of post-ERCP pancreatitis in these patients.
Conservative treatment preferred; consider intervention if no other cause for pancreatitis is found.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · PANCREAS DIVISUM | MACROAMYLASEMIA

items.title: Macroamylasemia: Understanding Elevated Serum Amylase Levels
items.content: Macroamylasemia involves the circulation of amylase in a large polymer form, leading to elevated serum and low urinary amylase levels. This condition is found in 1.5% of nonalcoholic adults in hospital settings and typically presents as an incidental finding without pancreatic or other organ diseases. However, it can be associated with conditions like cirrhosis or non-Hodgkin's lymphoma.
keyPoints: Macroamylasemia affects 1.5% of the nonalcoholic general adult hospital population.
Characterized by elevated serum amylase and low urinary amylase levels.
Usually incidental, not related to pancreatic or other organ diseases.
Associated with cirrhosis or non-Hodgkin's lymphoma in some cases.

items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · ☐ HEREDITARY PANCREATITIS | ANNULAR PANCREAS

items.question: What is a typical symptom of annular pancreas?
items.explanation: Symptoms of annular pancreas include postprandial fullness, epigastric pain, nausea, and vomiting.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · ☐ HEREDITARY PANCREATITIS | ANNULAR PANCREAS

items.question: Which statement is true regarding pancreas divisum and its association with pancreatitis?
options: Pancreas divisum predisposes the majority of patients to develop pancreatitis.
The combination of pancreas divisum and a small accessory orifice can result in dorsal duct obstruction.
ERCP cannulation of the dorsal duct is safe and does not increase the risk of post-ERCP pancreatitis.
Pancreatitis associated with pancreas divisum always requires endoscopic intervention.
items.explanation: The combination of pancreas divisum and a small accessory orifice can result in dorsal duct obstruction, potentially leading to pancreatitis.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · PANCREAS DIVISUM | MACROAMYLASEMIA

items.question: What is the prevalence of macroamylasemia in the nonalcoholic general adult hospital population?
items.explanation: The prevalence of macroamylasemia is 1.5% in the nonalcoholic general adult hospital population.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · PANCREAS DIVISUM | MACROAMYLASEMIA

items.question: Which condition can be documented by chromatography of serum?
items.explanation: The presence of macroamylase can be documented by chromatography of the serum.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · PANCREAS DIVISUM | MACROAMYLASEMIA

items.subtopic: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis | GENERAL CONSIDERATIONS | REGULATION OF PANCREATIC SECRETION | WATER AND ELECTROLYTE SECRETION
items.statement: CCK is the primary hormone responsible for water and bicarbonate secretion in pancreatic ductal cells.
items.explanation: Secretin, not CCK, primarily stimulates water and bicarbonate secretion from pancreatic ductal cells.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis | GENERAL CONSIDERATIONS | REGULATION OF PANCREATIC SECRETION | WATER AND ELECTROLYTE SECRETION

items.subtopic: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis | GENERAL CONSIDERATIONS | REGULATION OF PANCREATIC SECRETION | WATER AND ELECTROLYTE SECRETION
items.statement: The parasympathetic nervous system plays a significant role in the cephalic phase of pancreatic secretion.
items.explanation: The parasympathetic nervous system, via the vagus nerve, exerts significant control over pancreatic secretion during the cephalic phase.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis | GENERAL CONSIDERATIONS | REGULATION OF PANCREATIC SECRETION | WATER AND ELECTROLYTE SECRETION

items.statement: CCK receptors are present on human acinar cells.
items.explanation: In humans, there are no CCK receptors on acinar cells.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · ENZYME SECRETION

items.statement: Bile salts activate phospholipase A and cholesterol esterase.
items.explanation: Bile salts indeed activate both phospholipase A and cholesterol esterase.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · ENZYME SECRETION

items.subtopic: AUTOPROTECTION OF THE PANCREAS | ENTEROPANCREATIC AXIS AND FEEDBACK INHIBITION
items.statement: Loss of any protective mechanism against autodigestion can lead to acute pancreatitis.
items.explanation: Premature enzyme activation due to loss of protective mechanisms leads to autodigestion and acute pancreatitis.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · AUTOPROTECTION OF THE PANCREAS | ENTEROPANCREATIC AXIS AND FEEDBACK INHIBITION

items.statement: Perfusion with trypsin in the duodenal lumen increases plasma CCK levels.
items.explanation: Trypsin blunts the increase in plasma CCK levels, unlike phenylalanine which causes a prompt increase.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · AUTOPROTECTION OF THE PANCREAS | ENTEROPANCREATIC AXIS AND FEEDBACK INHIBITION

items.statement: Alcohol is the leading cause of acute pancreatitis in most series.
items.explanation: Gallstones are the leading cause, while alcohol is the second most common.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · GENERAL CONSIDERATIONS | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.statement: The incidence of acute pancreatitis in individuals with heavy alcohol use is high.
items.explanation: Despite heavy alcohol use, the incidence is surprisingly low at 5/100,000.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · GENERAL CONSIDERATIONS | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: ACTIVATION OF PANCREATIC ENZYMES IN THE PATHOGENESIS OF ACUTE PANCREATITIS
items.statement: The third phase of pancreatitis is characterized by the activation and effects of proteolytic enzymes on distant organs.
items.explanation: The third phase involves systemic effects due to activated proteolytic enzymes affecting distant organs.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · ACTIVATION OF PANCREATIC ENZYMES IN THE PATHOGENESIS OF ACUTE PANCREATITIS

items.subtopic: ACTIVATION OF PANCREATIC ENZYMES IN THE PATHOGENESIS OF ACUTE PANCREATITIS
items.statement: SPINK1 mutations are sufficient by themselves to cause acute pancreatitis.
items.explanation: Only PRSS1 mutations can precipitate acute pancreatitis without other risk factors; SPINK1 is a disease modifier.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · ACTIVATION OF PANCREATIC ENZYMES IN THE PATHOGENESIS OF ACUTE PANCREATITIS

items.statement: Jaundice is a frequent finding in acute pancreatitis.
items.explanation: Jaundice occurs infrequently and may be due to extrinsic compression or intraductal obstruction.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · Abdominal Pain

items.statement: Pleural effusion is present in 10–20% of patients with acute pancreatitis.
items.explanation: Pulmonary findings, including pleural effusion, occur in 10–20% of patients.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · Abdominal Pain

items.statement: Serum amylase levels return to normal within 3-7 days even if pancreatitis is ongoing.
items.explanation: Total serum amylase values tend to normalize after 3–7 days despite continuing pancreatitis.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · LABORATORY DATA

items.statement: Hyperbilirubinemia in acute pancreatitis is usually permanent.
items.explanation: Hyperbilirubinemia is typically transient and resolves within 4–7 days.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · LABORATORY DATA

items.statement: A threefold or greater elevation in serum lipase alone is sufficient to diagnose acute pancreatitis.
items.explanation: Two of the three criteria are needed: typical pain, elevated enzymes, and/or confirmatory imaging.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: Diabetic ketoacidosis can be differentiated from acute pancreatitis by normal pancreas imaging.
items.explanation: In diabetic ketoacidosis, pancreas imaging is typically normal.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: Prophylactic antibiotics are recommended for severe acute pancreatitis.
items.explanation: Prophylactic antibiotics are no longer recommended for severe acute pancreatitis.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · CLINICAL COURSE, DEFINITIONS, AND CLASSIFICATIONS

items.statement: Patients with extrapancreatic necrosis have a more favorable prognosis than those with pancreatic necrosis.
items.explanation: Those with only extrapancreatic necrosis have a more favorable prognosis.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · CLINICAL COURSE, DEFINITIONS, AND CLASSIFICATIONS

items.statement: All patients with acute pancreatitis require admission to an intensive care unit.
items.explanation: Only those who do not respond to aggressive fluid resuscitation should be considered for ICU admission.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · ACUTE PANCREATITIS MANAGEMENT

items.statement: Patients with acute pancreatitis are initially made NPO.
items.explanation: The patient is initially made NPO to minimize nutrient-induced stimulation of the pancreas.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · ACUTE PANCREATITIS MANAGEMENT

items.subtopic: Risk Factors for Increased Severity | Markers of Severity at Admission or Within 24 h (part 1)
items.statement: A BISAP score of 3 or more is associated with a substantially increased risk for in-hospital mortality.
items.explanation: The presence of three or more factors in the BISAP score is linked to an increased risk for in-hospital mortality.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · Risk Factors for Increased Severity | Markers of Severity at Admission or Within 24 h (part 1)

items.subtopic: Risk Factors for Increased Severity | Markers of Severity at Admission or Within 24 h (part 1)
items.statement: A rising BUN during hospitalization only indicates inadequate hydration.
items.explanation: A rising BUN is associated with both inadequate hydration and higher in-hospital mortality.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · Risk Factors for Increased Severity | Markers of Severity at Admission or Within 24 h (part 1)

items.subtopic: Markers of Severity at Admission or Within 24 h (part 2)
items.statement: Prophylactic antibiotics improve survival in necrotizing pancreatitis.
items.explanation: Prophylactic antibiotics do not lead to improved survival and may promote opportunistic fungal infections.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · Markers of Severity at Admission or Within 24 h (part 2)

items.subtopic: Markers of Severity at Admission or Within 24 h (part 2)
items.statement: Most acute pancreatic fluid collections evolve into pseudocysts.
items.explanation: Less than 10% of patients have persistent fluid collections after 4 weeks that would meet the definition of a pseudocyst.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · Markers of Severity at Admission or Within 24 h (part 2)

items.subtopic: Markers of Severity at Admission or Within 24 h (part 3) | RECURRENT ACUTE PANCREATITIS | PANCREATITIS IN PATIENTS WITH AIDS | ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
items.statement: Cholecystectomy should be performed during the initial hospitalization for acute gallstone pancreatitis with severe clinical severity.
items.explanation: The passage states that cholecystectomy should be performed during the initial hospitalization for mild, not severe, clinical severity.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · Markers of Severity at Admission or Within 24 h (part 3) | RECURRENT ACUTE PANCREATITIS | PANCREATITIS IN PATIENTS WITH AIDS | ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Markers of Severity at Admission or Within 24 h (part 3) | RECURRENT ACUTE PANCREATITIS | PANCREATITIS IN PATIENTS WITH AIDS | ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
items.statement: Smoking is an independent risk factor for chronic pancreatitis.
items.explanation: The passage indicates that smoking is an independent, dose-dependent risk factor for chronic pancreatitis.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · Markers of Severity at Admission or Within 24 h (part 3) | RECURRENT ACUTE PANCREATITIS | PANCREATITIS IN PATIENTS WITH AIDS | ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.statement: Patients with two CFTR mutations have a 40-fold increased risk of pancreatitis.
items.explanation: Patients with two CFTR mutations (compound heterozygotes) demonstrated a 40-fold increased risk of pancreatitis.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · ETIOLOGIC CONSIDERATIONS

items.statement: The presence of an N34S SPINK1 mutation alone increases the risk of pancreatitis by 900-fold.
items.explanation: A combination of two CFTR mutations and an N34S SPINK1 mutation increases the risk of pancreatitis 900-fold, not just the N34S SPINK1 mutation alone.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · ETIOLOGIC CONSIDERATIONS

items.statement: Elevated serum IgG4 levels have a high positive predictive value for diagnosing AIP.
items.explanation: Elevated serum IgG4 levels are supportive of the diagnosis but have a low positive predictive value when used in isolation.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · AUTOIMMUNE PANCREATITIS (TABLE 359-6) (part 1)

items.statement: AIP is commonly associated with idiopathic recurrent acute pancreatitis.
items.explanation: AIP is not a common cause of idiopathic recurrent acute pancreatitis.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · AUTOIMMUNE PANCREATITIS (TABLE 359-6) (part 1)

items.statement: Diabetic ketoacidosis and diabetic coma are common complications in patients with chronic pancreatitis-related diabetes.
items.explanation: Although most patients develop hyperglycemia due to insulin deficiency, diabetic ketoacidosis and diabetic coma are uncommon.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · AUTOIMMUNE PANCREATITIS (TABLE 359-6) (part 2)

items.statement: The lifetime prevalence of chronic pancreatitis-related diabetes exceeds 80%.
items.explanation: The lifetime prevalence of chronic pancreatitis-related diabetes indeed exceeds 80%.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · AUTOIMMUNE PANCREATITIS (TABLE 359-6) (part 2)

items.statement: Total pancreatectomy with autologous islet cell transplantation guarantees pain relief in all patients.
items.explanation: Some patients continue to have pain postoperatively, indicating that total pancreatectomy does not guarantee pain relief for all.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · Chronic Pancreatitis | STEATORRHEA | ABDOMINAL PAIN

items.statement: Celiac plexus block is recommended as a first-line treatment for chronic pancreatitis-related pain.
items.explanation: The use of celiac plexus block has conditional recommendations with very low quality of evidence and does not provide durable pain relief.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · Chronic Pancreatitis | STEATORRHEA | ABDOMINAL PAIN

items.statement: Hereditary pancreatitis is associated with a high cumulative incidence of pancreatic cancer (~10%).
items.explanation: Patients with hereditary pancreatitis have an increased risk of developing pancreatic cancer, with a cumulative incidence of about 10%.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · ☐ HEREDITARY PANCREATITIS | ANNULAR PANCREAS

items.statement: The treatment for annular pancreas is primarily medical management.
items.explanation: Surgical intervention, such as retrocolic duodenojejunostomy, is the treatment of choice for refractory symptoms in annular pancreas.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · ☐ HEREDITARY PANCREATITIS | ANNULAR PANCREAS

items.statement: Macroamylasemia is usually related to disease of the pancreas.
items.explanation: Macroamylasemia is typically an incidental finding and not related to pancreatic or other organ diseases.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · PANCREAS DIVISUM | MACROAMYLASEMIA

items.statement: ERCP/MRCP appearance of pancreas divisum can be mistaken for an obstructed main pancreatic duct due to a mass lesion.
items.explanation: The ERCP/MRCP appearance of pancreas divisum may resemble an obstructed main pancreatic duct secondary to a mass lesion.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · PANCREAS DIVISUM | MACROAMYLASEMIA

items.subtopic: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis | GENERAL CONSIDERATIONS | REGULATION OF PANCREATIC SECRETION | WATER AND ELECTROLYTE SECRETION
items.assertion: CCK potentiates the stimulatory effects of secretin on pancreatic secretion.
items.reason: CCK acts as a neuromodulator that markedly enhances the action of secretin.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis | GENERAL CONSIDERATIONS | REGULATION OF PANCREATIC SECRETION | WATER AND ELECTROLYTE SECRETION

items.assertion: Proteolytic enzymes are secreted as inactive zymogen precursors.
items.reason: This prevents autodigestion of the pancreas by ensuring activation occurs only in the duodenum.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why proteolytic enzymes are secreted as inactive zymogens.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · ENZYME SECRETION

items.assertion: Autodigestion in acute pancreatitis results from premature activation of trypsin.
items.reason: Premature activation of trypsin leads to the activation of other proteolytic and lipolytic enzymes within the pancreas.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · GENERAL CONSIDERATIONS | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.assertion: Drugs are a major cause of acute pancreatitis.
items.reason: Drugs can cause pancreatitis through hypersensitivity reactions or toxic metabolites.
items.explanation: The assertion is false as <2% of cases are drug-related, though the reason is true.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · GENERAL CONSIDERATIONS | ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: ACTIVATION OF PANCREATIC ENZYMES IN THE PATHOGENESIS OF ACUTE PANCREATITIS
items.assertion: Neutrophil depletion can reduce the severity of experimentally induced pancreatitis.
items.reason: Neutrophils contribute to trypsinogen activation and inflammation in acute pancreatitis.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining why neutrophil depletion reduces severity.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · ACTIVATION OF PANCREATIC ENZYMES IN THE PATHOGENESIS OF ACUTE PANCREATITIS

items.assertion: Elevations of alanine aminotransferase (ALT) >3× the upper limit of normal are associated with gallstone etiology in acute pancreatitis.
items.reason: Gallstones can obstruct the bile duct, leading to elevated liver enzymes including ALT.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the association.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · LABORATORY DATA

items.assertion: The severity of pancreatitis correlates with serum lipase levels.
items.reason: Serum lipase is more specific than amylase for diagnosing pancreatitis.
items.explanation: While serum lipase is more specific, there is no correlation between its elevation and the severity of pancreatitis.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · LABORATORY DATA

items.assertion: Mild acute pancreatitis is usually self-limited within 3–7 days.
items.reason: Most patients with interstitial acute pancreatitis have mild pancreatitis.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain why mild acute pancreatitis resolves quickly.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · CLINICAL COURSE, DEFINITIONS, AND CLASSIFICATIONS

items.assertion: CT imaging should be performed within the first 48 hours of admission in all cases of acute pancreatitis.
items.reason: Recent studies report overutilization of CT imaging within 72 hours for mild severity disease.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, as CT imaging is not recommended during the first 48 hours; the reason supports this.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · CLINICAL COURSE, DEFINITIONS, AND CLASSIFICATIONS

items.assertion: Early intravenous fluid resuscitation is critical in managing acute pancreatitis.
items.reason: It prevents systemic complications from the secondary systemic inflammatory response.
items.explanation: The reason correctly explains why early fluid resuscitation is important.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · ACUTE PANCREATITIS MANAGEMENT

items.subtopic: Risk Factors for Increased Severity | Markers of Severity at Admission or Within 24 h (part 1)
items.assertion: Patients with gallstone pancreatitis should be considered for cholecystectomy during the same admission if they have mild acute pancreatitis.
items.reason: Gallstone pancreatitis is associated with an increased risk of recurrence.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why patients with gallstone pancreatitis should consider cholecystectomy.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · Risk Factors for Increased Severity | Markers of Severity at Admission or Within 24 h (part 1)

items.subtopic: Risk Factors for Increased Severity | Markers of Severity at Admission or Within 24 h (part 1)
items.assertion: Patients with serum triglycerides >1000 mg/dL do not require any specific treatment.
items.reason: Hypertriglyceridemia is associated with acute pancreatitis.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because hypertriglyceridemia requires specific management, although the reason is true.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · Risk Factors for Increased Severity | Markers of Severity at Admission or Within 24 h (part 1)

items.assertion: A bridging pancreatic stent is effective in resolving pancreatic duct disruptions.
items.reason: Placement of a bridging pancreatic stent for at least 6 weeks resolves the leak with high efficacy.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · Markers of Severity at Admission or Within 24 h (part 2)

items.subtopic: Markers of Severity at Admission or Within 24 h (part 2)
items.assertion: Percutaneous fine-needle aspiration is widely used to diagnose infected pancreatic necrosis.
items.reason: This technique avoids contaminating sterile collections, which guides clinical decisions on antimicrobial therapy.
items.explanation: The assertion is false as the use of this technique varies and many institutions have abandoned it; however, the reason correctly explains why some avoid its use.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · Markers of Severity at Admission or Within 24 h (part 2)

items.assertion: Not all heavy consumers of alcohol develop chronic pancreatitis.
items.reason: Other factors, such as smoking, are likely required for the development and progression of the disease.
items.explanation: The passage explains that additional factors like smoking are needed alongside alcohol to develop chronic pancreatitis.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · Markers of Severity at Admission or Within 24 h (part 3) | RECURRENT ACUTE PANCREATITIS | PANCREATITIS IN PATIENTS WITH AIDS | ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.assertion: PSCs are stimulated by cytokines, oxidants, and growth factors.
items.reason: This stimulation leads to the destruction of normal collagen in pancreatic parenchyma.
items.explanation: While PSCs are indeed stimulated by these factors, the passage states that alcohol or additional stimuli lead to matrix metalloproteinase-mediated destruction of normal collagen.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · Markers of Severity at Admission or Within 24 h (part 3) | RECURRENT ACUTE PANCREATITIS | PANCREATITIS IN PATIENTS WITH AIDS | ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.assertion: The PRSS1 gene defect leads to chronic pancreatitis.
items.reason: The defect prevents the destruction of prematurely activated trypsin, causing continual activation of digestive enzymes within the gland.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why the PRSS1 gene defect leads to chronic pancreatitis.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · ETIOLOGIC CONSIDERATIONS

items.assertion: CFTR mutations alone can lead to pancreatitis as an autosomal recessive disease.
items.reason: The presence of CFTR mutations is common in the general population.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because it's unclear if CFTR mutation alone leads to pancreatitis as an autosomal recessive disease, while the reason is true.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · ETIOLOGIC CONSIDERATIONS

items.assertion: Type 1 AIP often involves other organs.
items.reason: Type 1 AIP is part of IgG4-related disease, which affects multiple organs.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · AUTOIMMUNE PANCREATITIS (TABLE 359-6) (part 1)

items.assertion: Patients with chronic pancreatitis typically have focal pancreatic enlargement.
items.reason: CT imaging demonstrates abnormalities in the majority of patients with either diffuse or focal enlargement during active disease.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason does not specifically address focal enlargement as typical.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · AUTOIMMUNE PANCREATITIS (TABLE 359-6) (part 1)

items.assertion: Endoscopic ultrasonography alone is specific enough to diagnose early chronic pancreatitis.
items.reason: EUS may show positive features in patients with diabetes, a history of cigarette smoking, or even normal aging individuals.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; EUS is not specific enough for diagnosing early chronic pancreatitis alone.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · AUTOIMMUNE PANCREATITIS (TABLE 359-6) (part 2)

items.assertion: Patients with hereditary PRSS1 or tropical pancreatitis have an increased risk for pancreatic cancer.
items.reason: These patients are compared to other forms of chronic pancreatitis, which have a lower risk.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · AUTOIMMUNE PANCREATITIS (TABLE 359-6) (part 2)

items.assertion: Endoscopic treatment options for chronic pancreatitis include sphincterotomy and pancreatic duct stenting.
items.reason: These treatments are directed at managing pain by addressing structural issues in the pancreatic duct.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why these endoscopic treatments are used.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · Chronic Pancreatitis | STEATORRHEA | ABDOMINAL PAIN

items.assertion: Pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy is effective in completely correcting steatorrhea.
items.reason: Enzyme therapy usually brings diarrhea under control and restores absorption of fat to an acceptable level.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because complete correction of steatorrhea with enzyme therapy is uncommon, despite its benefits.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · Chronic Pancreatitis | STEATORRHEA | ABDOMINAL PAIN

items.assertion: Patients with hereditary pancreatitis often develop diabetes mellitus.
items.reason: The recurrent episodes of acute pancreatitis can lead to pancreatic damage and dysfunction, resulting in diabetes.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why patients with hereditary pancreatitis often develop diabetes.
items.reference: Acute and Chronic Pancreatitis · ☐ HEREDITARY PANCREATITIS | ANNULAR PANCREAS
